expectations.text: The code uses .map() or .starmap() for synchronous fan-out and in-process result collection
expectations.evidence: solution.py line 134: `resize_to_thumbnail.map(image_payloads, order_outputs=True, return_exceptions=True)` — uses .map() directly on the Modal function.

expectations.text: The code does not use .spawn() since the caller needs results back immediately
expectations.evidence: No call to .spawn() or .spawn_map() appears anywhere in solution.py. The transcript also explicitly notes: 'Workflow: .map() with @app.local_entrypoint() — caller collects all 5,000 results in-process.'

expectations.text: The code includes a @app.function or @app.cls decorated resize function
expectations.evidence: solution.py lines 40–51: `@app.function(image=remote_image, max_containers=100, timeout=60, retries=1)` decorates `resize_to_thumbnail`.

expectations.text: The code demonstrates collecting all results (e.g., iterating the .map() generator or list(...))
expectations.evidence: solution.py line 133: `results = list(resize_to_thumbnail.map(...))` explicitly materializes the generator, then iterates `results` in a for loop on line 146.

expectations.text: The code sets or acknowledges max_containers or concurrency limits where relevant
expectations.evidence: solution.py line 44: `max_containers=100` is set on the @app.function decorator. Comments explain the rationale: '5,000 images / 100 containers = ~50 images per container at full saturation.'

expectations.text: The code uses a local_entrypoint or with app.run(): to orchestrate the 5,000 inputs
expectations.evidence: solution.py line 108: `@app.local_entrypoint()` decorates `main()`, which generates 5,000 synthetic images and submits the batch via .map().

expectations.text: The code uses .spawn() to submit the job asynchronously
expectations.evidence: solution.py line 93: `call: modal.FunctionCall = remote_fn.spawn(payload)` inside the POST /jobs handler. The inline comment reads: '.spawn() submits the job and returns a FunctionCall immediately. The web server is free the moment spawn() returns.'

expectations.text: The code captures and returns a job ID or FunctionCall handle to the caller
expectations.evidence: solution.py lines 97-102: `job_id: str = call.object_id` is extracted from the FunctionCall and returned in a `JobSubmitResponse` with field `job_id`. The HTTP response body carries this stable string ID to the caller.

expectations.text: The code demonstrates how to poll or retrieve the result using the job ID (e.g., fc.get() or polling fc.get(timeout=0))
expectations.evidence: solution.py lines 114-121: `call = modal.FunctionCall.from_id(job_id)` rehydrates the handle, then `call.get(timeout=0)` is used with a TimeoutError catch to return status='pending' if still running. The local_test() entrypoint (lines 143-151) shows the full poll loop pattern using the same from_id + get(timeout=0) approach.

expectations.text: The code deploys the function with modal deploy or uses Function.from_name() so the web server can reference a stable remote function
expectations.evidence: solution.py line 78: `return modal.Function.from_name("detached-job-worker", "process_job")` inside `_get_remote_function()`. The module docstring instructs: 'Deploy the worker first: modal deploy solution.py'. Transcript Step 2 also confirms this pattern was drawn directly from the reference document.

expectations.text: The code does not block the web server request handler waiting for completion
expectations.evidence: The POST /jobs handler calls `.spawn()` which returns immediately (no blocking wait). The GET /jobs/{job_id} poll handler uses `call.get(timeout=0)` — timeout=0 is explicitly non-blocking and raises TimeoutError if the job is not yet done, which is caught and returns status='pending' without waiting.

expectations.text: The response explains the difference between .spawn() and .map() in this context
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 4 explicitly states: 'No .map: .map blocks the caller until all results are collected — the opposite of what is needed here.' and 'No .spawn_map: The task is single-job submission with a returned job ID — .spawn_map is for fire-and-forget fan-out with external result sinks, which does not match.' The solution.py inline comment also notes that .spawn() returns immediately while the web server remains free, implying the blocking nature of alternatives.

expectations.text: The code uses @modal.batched decorator on the transcription function
expectations.evidence: solution.py line 52: `@modal.batched(max_batch_size=16, wait_ms=200)` decorates the `transcribe` method of `WhisperBatchTranscriber`.

expectations.text: The code sets a batch_size appropriate for GPU memory and throughput
expectations.evidence: solution.py line 52 sets `max_batch_size=16`. Transcript Step 3 justifies this: 'The Whisper base model on a T4 (16 GB VRAM) can comfortably process 16 audio clips simultaneously.' Inline comments in solution.py note how to scale up or down for different model sizes.

expectations.text: The code sets wait_ms to control how long to wait for a batch to fill
expectations.evidence: solution.py line 52: `wait_ms=200` is set in the `@modal.batched` decorator. The docstring explains: 'queue inputs for up to 200 ms to build a larger batch. Tune lower (e.g. 50 ms) for latency-sensitive workloads; higher (e.g. 500 ms) for pure throughput.'

expectations.text: The decorated function receives a list of inputs and returns a list of results (matching length)
expectations.evidence: solution.py line 53: signature is `def transcribe(self, audio_bytes_list: list[bytes]) -> list[str]`. Lines 96-99 enforce the contract with an assertion: `assert len(results) == len(audio_bytes_list)`.

expectations.text: The code uses GPU resources (gpu= parameter) on the batched function
expectations.evidence: solution.py lines 27-32: `@app.cls(image=image, gpu="T4", ...)` — the GPU is specified on the class decorator that wraps the batched method, which is the correct place for the gpu= parameter when using @app.cls.

expectations.text: The response explains or demonstrates how callers invoke the batched function (individual calls that get coalesced)
expectations.evidence: solution.py lines 107-139 show two caller patterns in `main()`: (1) `transcriber.transcribe.remote(audio_bytes)` for a single item, (2) `transcriber.transcribe.map(audio_payloads)` for a stream. Comments explicitly state '.map() submits each item individually; @modal.batched coalesces them server-side into GPU batches automatically.' A third pattern using `modal.Cls.from_name()` is shown at lines 146-156.

expectations.text: The code uses .map() or .starmap() for synchronous fan-out and in-process result collection
expectations.evidence: solution.py uses .map() twice: 'generate_image.map(range(total_images), order_outputs=True)' and 'resize_to_thumbnail.map(image_bytes_list, kwargs={...}, order_outputs=True, return_exceptions=True)'. Both calls fan out work across Modal containers and collect results.

expectations.text: The code does not use .spawn() since the caller needs results back immediately
expectations.evidence: No call to .spawn() appears anywhere in solution.py. All remote invocations go through .map() which blocks until results are available.

expectations.text: The code includes a @app.function or @app.cls decorated resize function
expectations.evidence: solution.py line 53: '@app.function(image=image, cpu=1, memory=512)' decorates 'def resize_to_thumbnail(image_data: bytes, thumbnail_size: tuple = (128, 128)) -> dict'.

expectations.text: The code demonstrates collecting all results (e.g., iterating the .map() generator or list(...))
expectations.evidence: Both .map() calls are wrapped in list(): 'image_bytes_list = list(generate_image.map(...))' and 'results = list(resize_to_thumbnail.map(...))'. The results list is then iterated to separate successes from exceptions.

expectations.text: The code sets or acknowledges max_containers or concurrency limits where relevant
expectations.evidence: No max_containers, concurrency, or allow_concurrent_inputs parameter is set on any @app.function decorator. The decorators only specify cpu, memory, and timeout. The transcript and code comments do not discuss concurrency limits either.

expectations.text: The code uses a local_entrypoint or with app.run(): to orchestrate the 5,000 inputs
expectations.evidence: solution.py line 110: '@app.local_entrypoint()' decorates 'def main():', which orchestrates both the image generation and thumbnail processing .map() calls over 5,000 inputs.

expectations.text: The code uses .spawn() to submit the job asynchronously
expectations.evidence: solution.py line 93: `call: modal.functions.FunctionCall = long_running_job.spawn(body)` in the POST /jobs handler. Also used in the local_entrypoint at line 150.

expectations.text: The code captures and returns a job ID or FunctionCall handle to the caller
expectations.evidence: solution.py line 95: `job_id: str = call.object_id` is extracted from the FunctionCall and returned in the HTTP 202 response as `{"job_id": job_id, "status": "queued", ...}`.

expectations.text: The code demonstrates how to poll or retrieve the result using the job ID (e.g., fc.get() or polling fc.get(timeout=0))
expectations.evidence: solution.py lines 114-117: the GET /jobs/{job_id} endpoint calls `modal.functions.FunctionCall.from_id(job_id)` then `call.get(timeout=0)`, catching `modal.exception.TimeoutError` to return a 202 'running' status. The local_entrypoint also shows a polling loop with the same pattern (lines 155-162).

expectations.text: The code deploys the function with modal deploy or uses Function.from_name() so the web server can reference a stable remote function
expectations.evidence: Both `long_running_job` and `web_server` are defined on the same `modal.App` object and the Notes section (line 186) instructs `modal deploy solution.py`. The web server references `long_running_job` directly by name within the same app, which is valid for a co-deployed setup. Deployment instructions are explicit.

expectations.text: The code does not block the web server request handler waiting for completion
expectations.evidence: POST /jobs uses `.spawn()` which returns immediately (non-blocking). GET /jobs/{job_id} uses `call.get(timeout=0)` which raises `TimeoutError` immediately if incomplete — it never blocks. The transcript Step 3 explicitly confirms: 'get(timeout=0) ensures the web server is never blocked waiting'.

expectations.text: The response explains the difference between .spawn() and .map() in this context
expectations.evidence: Neither solution.py nor the transcript mentions `.map()` at all. The solution explains `.spawn()`, `.get()`, and `.from_id()` thoroughly but makes no comparison to `.map()` or any other Modal calling convention.

expectations.text: The code uses @modal.batched decorator on the transcription function
expectations.evidence: solution.py line 89: `@modal.batched(max_batch_size=16, wait_ms=500)` applied directly to the `transcribe` method on `WhisperBatchTranscriber`.

expectations.text: The code sets a batch_size appropriate for GPU memory and throughput
expectations.evidence: max_batch_size=16 is set and justified in the transcript (Step 3) and code comments: 'chosen to fit 16 simultaneous transcription tasks within typical A10G memory; adjust down if very long audio files cause OOM.' The A10G has 24 GB VRAM, making 16 a reasonable upper bound for audio transcription workloads.

expectations.text: The code sets wait_ms to control how long to wait for a batch to fill
expectations.evidence: solution.py line 89: `wait_ms=500` is set on `@modal.batched`. Transcript Step 3 explains: '500 ms keeps batch utilization high without adding more than half a second of queuing latency per request.'

expectations.text: The decorated function receives a list of inputs and returns a list of results (matching length)
expectations.evidence: solution.py line 90: signature is `def transcribe(self, audio_bytes_batch: list[bytes]) -> list[str]`. The body appends one result per input element, and the docstring states: 'len(result) == len(audio_bytes_batch) is always guaranteed by Modal.'

expectations.text: The code uses GPU resources (gpu= parameter) on the batched function
expectations.evidence: solution.py lines 46-51: `@app.cls(gpu="A10G", ...)` is set on the class containing the batched method. The GPU is attached at the class level, which is the correct Modal pattern for @app.cls methods — individual methods cannot independently declare gpu= when using @app.cls.

expectations.text: The response explains or demonstrates how callers invoke the batched function (individual calls that get coalesced)
expectations.evidence: Two caller patterns are demonstrated. Pattern 1 (solution.py lines 232): `results = list(transcriber.transcribe.map(audio_bytes_list))`. Pattern 2 (lines 268-269): `futures = [transcriber.transcribe.spawn(audio) for audio in audio_bytes_list]; return [f.get() for f in futures]`. Both are explained in transcript Step 3 and the main() docstring.
